name: Kabyle

allophone_rules.notes: Spirantization of /b/ is blocked after a homorganic nasal /m/ (e.g., zembil 'basket' → [zəmbil], not *[zəmβil]). Kossmann & Stroomer (1997) p.469 (illustrated there for the Beni Said Riffian dialect; extended here to Kabyle by analogy — see source notes). Corroborated for Kabyle (Chemini) by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022): /nβ/ → [mb]. Also directly attested for /mβ/ → [mb] itself, Table 25/27-28 — and unlike the /t,d/-after-/m/ cases below, this occlusivization is reported CONSTANT across all four surveyed varieties (Chemini, Aït Mengellat, Boghni, Makouda), so no dialect-scope caveat is needed here.

Spirantization of /d/ is blocked after a homorganic nasal /n/ (e.g., anda 'where' → [anda], not *[anða]). Kossmann & Stroomer (1997) p.469 (Riffian example; extended to Kabyle by analogy). Corroborated for Chemini Kabyle by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) Table 25 (/nð/ → [nd], e.g. anda 'where').

Spirantization of /t/ is blocked after a homorganic nasal /n/ (e.g., anta 'which one (f.)' → [anta], not *[anθa]; also corpus-attested: anteɣ). Kossmann & Stroomer (1997) p.469 (Riffian example; extended to Kabyle by analogy). Corroborated for Kabyle (Chemini) by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022): /nθ/ → [nt]. SPEAKER NOTE 2026-07-17: anta is a native Kabyle word — 'which one', feminine of anwa 'which one (m.)'. An earlier revision of this note glossed it as 'you' and a later one dismissed the word as Arabic on that basis; the word is native, only the gloss was wrong.

Spirantization of word-final /t/ is blocked after /l/ (e.g., tamellalt 'egg' → [θaməllalt], not *[θaməllaθ]). SCOPE NOTE 2026-07-17: Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) attest /lθ,lð/ → [lt,ld] as a GENERAL cluster rule in Chemini Kabyle (not restricted to word-final position); this spec conservatively keeps the rule word-final-only pending verification that the same blocking holds word-medially in standard Kabyle.

Spirantization of /dˤ/ is blocked after a homorganic nasal /n/. NOTE: this rule is NOT covered by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022), whose study only treats the non-emphatic non-strident fricatives /β, θ, ð, ç, ʝ/; it rests on Kossmann & Stroomer (1997)/analogy and native-speaker judgment only.
Spirantization of /d/ is blocked after /l/ (e.g., Ldi 'open' → [ldi], not [lði]).

Spirantization of /t/ is blocked after /m/ (e.g., tasemmumt → [tasemmumt], not [tasemmumθ]). DIALECT SCOPE NOTE 2026-07-17: this reflects the Chemini and Aït Mengellat pattern (/mθ/ → [mt]) per Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) Table 27-28. Boghni and Makouda do NOT show this occlusivization there (spirantization survives: [mθ]); this spec follows the Chemini/Aït Mengellat pattern and does not model dialectal variation across Kabyle varieties.

NEW RULE, added 2026-07-17 — flag for review before merging. Spirantization of /d/ is blocked after /m/ (mirrors KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_T_AFTER_M): Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) Table 25/27-28 attest /mð/ → [md] in Chemini Kabyle (e.g. θamda 'pond') and in Aït Mengellat. As with the /t/ case, Boghni and Makouda do NOT occlusivize here (spirantization survives: [mð]); this spec follows the Chemini/Aït Mengellat pattern. This rule was previously ABSENT from the spec, meaning /d/ after /m/ would incorrectly spirantize to [ð] under the prior version — this addition closes that gap for consistency with the sibling /t/ rule, but has not yet been independently native-speaker-verified the way the other rules in this file have.

Spirantization of /k/ is blocked after any consonant: /k/ → [ç] only post-vocalically or word-initially (native-speaker judgment, 2026-07). The sonorant cases (after /r, l, n, m/) are corroborated for Chemini Kabyle by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022, RFP Porto): Table (13)/(6) shows /ç/ occlusivizes to [k] after ALL FOUR sonorants r, l, n, m — the only one of the five non-strident fricatives with unanimous occlusivization across all sonorant contexts, consistent with this rule's categorical 'blocked after any consonant' formulation. Geminate contexts included 2026-07-17: corpus evidence (nettkel, lettkal — k after ⟨tt⟩ /tː/) showed the singleton-only list let k wrongly spirantize after geminates.

Spirantization of /ɡ/ is blocked after any consonant EXCEPT /m/: /ɡ/ → [ʝ] post-vocalically, word-initially, and after /m/ — Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) document /mʝ/ → [mʝ] in Chemini Kabyle, the one environment where the categorical post-consonant blocking (native-speaker judgment, 2026-07) does not apply. VOWEL-CONDITIONING NOTE 2026-07-17: this /mʝ/ exception is itself NOT unconditional — the paper's Table 27-28 shows /mʝ/ → [mʝ] only before /ə, i, a/; before /u/, /ʝ/ instead DEBUCCALIZES to [mh] (e.g. [mʝ] contexts elsewhere but [mh] before u), and this /u/-conditioned outcome holds across Chemini, Boghni, and Makouda alike. The following-vowel conditioning is NOT modeled by this rule engine (no mechanism for a following-vowel-sensitive exception to a preceding-consonant rule) — see KNOWN LIMITS. Sonorant cases after /r, l, n/ corroborated by the same study: Table (13)/(6) shows /ʝ/ occlusivizing after r, l, n but NOT after m — the asymmetry this rule already encodes correctly for the default (non-u) case.

Spirantization of the lax velar stop, with palatal shift: /k/ → [ç] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.469, Riffian; Karaoui et al. 2024 list [ç] in the Kabyle fricative inventory). Applies post-vocalically and word-initially; blocked after any consonant (KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_K_AFTER_CONSONANT). The [ʃ] variant reported for Beni Said Riffian (K&S 1997 p.469) does NOT occur in Kabyle (native-speaker judgment, 2026-07) and is not modelled. Geminate ⟨kk⟩ = /kː/ stays a stop.

Spirantization of the lax (non-geminate) bilabial stop: /b/ → [β]. Kossmann & Stroomer (1997), Berber Phonology, p.466 (general Berber statement) and p.469 (Riffian illustration). The tense (geminate) counterpart, written ⟨bb⟩ and mapped to /bː/, is a distinct phoneme and is NOT targeted by this rule (p.466: 'Spirantization never affects tense consonants').
Spirantization of the lax dental stop: /d/ → [ð] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.466, general Berber; p.469, Riffian illustration). E.g. adrar 'mountain' → [aðrar]. Geminate ⟨dd⟩ = /dː/ stays a stop.

Spirantization of the lax voiceless dental stop: /t/ → [θ] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.466-469). E.g. tafat 'light' → [θafaθ], tawwurt 'door' → [θawwurθ], tameṭṭut 'woman' → [θametˤːθ]. Geminate ⟨tt⟩ = /tː/ stays a stop. Applies word-initially before ALL vowels, including /i/ — native-speaker verified 2026-07-17: tifinaɣ, tilufa, tilelli, tikwal all begin [θi-]; consistent with the 2026-07-16 verification table (tibḥirt, ticcert, tiddi, tigawt). Intervocalic -ti- remains to be verified.

Spirantization of the lax velar stop: /ɡ/ → [ʝ], post-vocalically and word-initially; blocked after any consonant EXCEPT /m/ (KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_G_AFTER_CONSONANT): Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) document /mʝ/ → [mʝ] in Chemini Kabyle (see that rule's notes for the /u/-conditioned [mh] exception). The Kossmann & Stroomer (1997) p.469 example argaz → [arʝaz] is Riffian-only; in Kabyle, argaz surfaces with a stop [arɡaz]. Geminate ⟨gg⟩ = /ɡː/ stays a stop in all contexts.
Spirantization of the lax emphatic (pharyngealized) dental stop: /dˤ/ → [ðˤ] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.468). Geminate ⟨ḍḍ⟩ = /dˤː/ stays a stop.

/ɣ/ is realized as the uvular fricative [ʁ] — the Kabyle norm in all positions, including word-finally (e.g., tifinaɣ → [θifinɑʁ], bbeɣ 'to dive' → [bːəʁ]). Sources: the Kabyle consonant chart (ɣ in the uvular column as [ʁ]) and the IPA [ʁ] reference article's word-final Kabyle example [bːəʁ]; consistent with the uvular behaviour of /ɣ/ in the a-backing rules. No word-final devoicing to [χ] is documented for Kabyle. The broad velar symbol [ɣ] is retained as a documented variant in the allophones table.
Geminate ⟨ɣɣ⟩ = /ɣː/ is realized as long uvular [ʁː]; see KAB_GH_UVULAR_REALIZATION.

Vowel backing: /a/ → [ɑ] before emphatic and uvular consonants, singleton or geminate (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.463-464: backing/pharyngealization is an automatic, place-conditioned spreading effect, not gemination-sensitive — consistent with KAB_N_VELAR_ASSIM's treatment of velar/uvular geminates below). Fixed in this revision: geminate uvulars/emphatics (χː, ɣː, qː, tˤː, etc.) were previously missing, causing words like axxam 'house' to incorrectly surface as [æχːæm] instead of [ɑχːɑm].
Vowel backing: /a/ → [ɑ] after emphatic and uvular consonants, singleton or geminate (see KAB_A_BACKING).
Default realization of /a/ as [æ] when not backed by emphatic/uvular context (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.463; Wikipedia).
Vowel lowering: /i/ → [ɪ] in closed syllables (before consonant clusters).
Vowel lowering: /u/ → [ʊ] in closed syllables (before consonant clusters).
Nasal place assimilation: /n/ → [ŋ] before a dorsal (velar/uvular) consonant. Corroborated for Chemini Kabyle by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022): /nç, nʝ/ → [ŋk, ŋɡ].
Nasal place assimilation: /n/ → [m] before a labial consonant.
Nasal place assimilation: /n/ → [ɲ] before a palatal consonant /j/.

notes: Kabyle (Taqbaylit), Northern Berber (Amazigh) language of Kabylia, north-eastern Algeria, ~5-7 million speakers (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997, Berber Phonology, p.461). INPUT CONTRACT: this spec expects text in the standardised Berber LATIN alphabet (the tamaziɣt/INALCO orthography descending from Mammeri's conventions, used by the Kabyle Wikipedia, the press, and Naït-Zerrad's teaching grammars). Tifinagh (the neo-Tifinagh abjad-alphabet) is a secondary script for Kabyle and is NOT modelled here; a Tifinagh text must be transliterated to the Latin orthography first. Hallmark grapheme->IPA correspondences: c=/ʃ/, č=/t͡ʃ/, ǧ=/d͡ʒ/, j=/ʒ/, ɣ=/ɣ/, deterministically realised uvular [ʁ] (the Kabyle norm, all positions; broad [ɣ] documented as variant), x=/χ/, ḥ=/ħ/, ɛ=/ʕ/, q=/q/, and the emphatic (pharyngealized) series ṛ ṣ ḍ ṭ ẓ = /rˤ sˤ dˤ tˤ zˤ/ (Proto-Berber had only ḍ and ẓ pharyngealized; the rest are Arabic-integrated — Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.464). ORTHOGRAPHIC NOTE — THE 'v' QUESTION: native Kabyle words are written with ⟨b⟩ only; /b/ surfaces as [b] (post-consonantal, e.g. zembil [zəmbil]), [bː] (geminate, e.g. abba 'father' [æbːæ], bbeɣ [bːəʁ]) or the lax BILABIAL fricative [β] (e.g. abrid 'path' [æβrið]) — never labiodental [v]. Spellings like ⟨avrid⟩ mishear the bilabial [β]; [v] is absent from the native inventory (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997, Kabyle consonant chart). The ⟨v⟩→[v] and ⟨p⟩→[p] mappings in this spec serve unassimilated loanwords and foreign names only (extended orthography). Three phonemic vowels /a i u/ with wide context-dependent realisation (a:[a~ɑ~æ], i:[e~i], u:[o~u]; Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.463); ⟨e⟩ writes the epenthetic schwa [ə], which is largely predictable from syllable structure / sonority and mostly non-phonemic (ibid. p.463 §23.4.2). THE SIGNATURE FEATURE is SPIRANTIZATION (modelled as allophone_rules): the lax (non-geminate) stops b d t k g ḍ surface as the fricatives [β ð θ ç ʝ ðˤ], while the tense (geminate) stops — written by doubling the letter (bb dd tt kk gg ḍḍ) and mapped to the distinct long phonemes /bː dː tː kː ɡː dˤː/ — remain stops and are never spirantized (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.466: 'Spirantization never affects tense consonants'; the lax:tense opposition is durational — lax consonants are 'always realized shorter than their tense counterparts', p.465). Because the geminates are represented as separate long phonemes, the spirantization rules are naturally gemination-conditioned: they target /b d t k ɡ dˤ/ but not /bː dː tː kː ɡː dˤː/. e.g. igenni 'sky' → [iʝənni], adrar 'mountain' → [aðrar]. SOURCING CAVEAT: the worked spirantization examples and the homorganic-nasal blocking rule on pp.468-469 are given in the source specifically for the Beni Said Riffian dialect (§23.7.1), not for Kabyle directly — the source's only Kabyle-specific statement is the general remark (§23.5.1, p.466) that spirantization 'reaches its culminating points in Riffian and Kabyle.' These rules are extended to Kabyle here by analogy between the two dialects, not from a Kabyle-specific citation. CURRENT REVISION (2026-07-17): (a) REMOVED KAB_SPIRANT_K_ALT and the [ʃ] allophone of /k/ — the k→ʃ value is given in K&S 1997 p.469 for Beni Said Riffian only; per native-speaker judgment it never occurs in Kabyle. (b) /t/ before /i/: a KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_T_BEFORE_I rule was added and then REMOVED on 2026-07-17 after speaker re-verification — word-initial /t/ before /i/ is ALWAYS [θ] (tifinaɣ, tilufa, tilelli, tikwal); the 2026-07-16 table stands. Intervocalic -ti- (asiti, itiknikanen, tirmitin) is the residual open item. (c) GENERALIZED k/ɡ blocking to KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_K_AFTER_CONSONANT / KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_G_AFTER_CONSONANT: k → [ç] and ɡ → [ʝ] only post-vocalically and word-initially, stops after any consonant (native-speaker judgment), with the single exception /mʝ/ → [mʝ] from Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022). The sonorant cases (k after r/l/n/m; ɡ after r/l/n) are corroborated by that study (Chemini Kabyle). (d) CORRECTED the Bedar–Quellec–Tifrit reference — the study is real but was miscited (wrong author first names, spurious 'BLS 50' venue and eScholarship link, unverifiable 'four varieties/Table 6' claims); the correct reference is Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022), RFP 2022 Porto, HAL hal-03691265. (e) ADDED KAB_GH_UVULAR_REALIZATION (+geminate): /ɣ/ → [ʁ] as default output, per source check 2026-07-17 (Kabyle consonant chart: ɣ = uvular [ʁ]; word-final example bbeɣ → [bːəʁ]; no final devoicing documented). (f) Fixed broken rule examples (zembil for mb-blocking; snat for nt-blocking), completed the allophones table for all phoneme targets (tˤ, dˤː, tˤː and all geminates), and extended KAB_N_VELAR_ASSIM to the geminate uvulars χː/ɣː. Earlier revisions added the geminate graphemes (xx, ww, yy, pp, jj, hh), v→/v/, the vowel-backing rules extended to geminate uvulars/emphatics (axxam → [ɑχːɑm]), palatal nasal assimilation /n/→[ɲ], and the homorganic-nasal plus /l/ and /m/ blocking rules for b/d/t/ḍ. FULL-TEXT VERIFICATION AGAINST Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) (2026-07-17, after obtaining full paper text): confirmed /nβ/→[mb], /nθ,nð/→[nt,nd], /nç,nʝ/→[ŋk,ŋɡ]; confirmed sonorant-context corroboration for k (occlusivizes after all of r,l,n,m) and g (occlusivizes after r,l,n but not m). TWO CORRECTIONS made from this full-text check: (i) the /mʝ/→[mʝ] exception is vowel-conditioned, not blanket — it holds before /ə,i,a/ but /ʝ/ instead debuccalizes to [mh] before /u/ (Table 27-28), an environment this rule engine cannot express (no following-vowel-sensitive exception mechanism) — documented as a known limit; (ii) KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_T_AFTER_M reflects the Chemini/Aït Mengellat pattern specifically — Boghni and Makouda do not occlusivize /t,d/ after /m/ per the same table, so this rule (and its new sibling KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_D_AFTER_M, added the same day to close a previously-unmodeled gap) are dialect-particular choices, not general-Kabyle facts. PENDING SPEAKER CHECKS (2026-07-17): intervocalic -ti- in asiti, susiti ([t] or [θ]?); sk-clusters of the askar type — spec yields [sk] categorically, but one 2026-07-16 table entry had [asçar]; if confirmed without an epenthetic schwa, the k rule needs an /s/ refinement; acki — [æʃki] or [akki]? SPEAKER RULINGS 2026-07-17: tirmitin → [θɪrmiθin] — BOTH t's spirantize (word-initial and intervocalic), confirming the categorical intervocalic rule in native words; itiknikanen (French loan ← techniciens) keeps stop [t] word-internally — a loanword exception the rule engine cannot express (no lexical-exception mechanism), documented here, not modelled; tetwitiḍ dropped from the verification set (word unknown to the speaker). KNOWN LIMITS (documented, not modelled): (1) morphophonological gemination alternations (geminated ɣ surfaces as qq, geminated w as bb, geminated y as gg) are lexical/morphological and are not derivable from the Latin spelling; (2) word-boundary sandhi (n+w → bb/pp, d+t → ts) is not modelled; (3) full vowel allophony (i→e, u→o) is only partially covered; (4) Kabyle stress is largely non-distinctive and predictable, and is not marked here; (5) recent loanwords (especially French technical loans such as itiknikanen ← techniciens) may escape spirantization and keep their stops — the spec models the native phonology categorically; (6) NEW 2026-07-17: /mʝ/ before /u/ surfaces as debuccalized [mh] rather than [mʝ] — not modelled, no following-vowel-conditioning mechanism on preceding-consonant rules exists in this engine; (7) NEW 2026-07-17: post-/m/ occlusivization of /t,d/ (KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_T_AFTER_M, KAB_BLOCK_SPIRANT_D_AFTER_M) is dialect-particular (Chemini/Aït Mengellat pattern); Boghni/Makouda are not modelled as an alternative. Labiovelarized kʷ gʷ are usually left unwritten in the standard orthography and are not modelled.

ancestors.notes: Immediate Glottolog subgroup (kaby1244): Afro-Asiatic > Berber > Kabyle-Atlas Berber > Kabyle. Traditionally within Northern Berber.
Arabic adstrate/superstrate: heavy lexical borrowing and the source of most of Kabyle's non-Proto-Berber emphatics and pharyngeals (ḥ /ħ/, ɛ /ʕ/, ṣ /sˤ/, ṭ /tˤ/), per Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.464.

sources.author: Kossmann, Maarten G. & Stroomer, Harry J.
sources.title: Berber Phonology
sources.publisher: In A. S. Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa, vol. 1, Eisenbrauns
sources.notes: PRIMARY source, read in full (verified directly, including a re-check of p.469). p.461: Kabyle ~7 million speakers. p.463 §23.4.1: three vowels a i u and their context realisations; §23.4.2 schwa placement by structure/sonority. p.464 §23.4.3.1: pharyngealization described as an automatic spreading feature under the influence of a pharyngealized phoneme; Proto-Berber only ḍ ẓ pharyngealized. p.465 §23.4.3.1.2: lax vs tense opposition, lax always shorter than tense (geminate). p.466 §23.5.1: general Berber statement that spirantization 'reaches its culminating points in Riffian and Kabyle' and that 'Spirantization never affects tense consonants.' IMPORTANT CAVEAT: the chapter has NO dedicated Kabyle phonology section — only Tashelhit (§23.6), Riffian (§23.7), and Touareg (§23.8) are treated in detail. The worked spirantization examples and values (b→β, d→ð, g→ʝ/ɣ, k→ʃ/ç) and the homorganic-nasal spirantization-blocking rule (illustrated with mb/nd/nt clusters) all appear on pp.468-469 under §23.7.1, specifically for the Beni Said Riffian dialect, and are extended to Kabyle in this spec by analogy rather than by direct citation. Re-checked p.469 specifically for a claimed /l/,/r/,/n/ blocking rule for /k/,/ɡ/ spirantization: NOT FOUND — the only blocking condition stated there is the homorganic-nasal rule; the generalized post-consonant blocking rules for k/ɡ in this spec therefore rest on native-speaker judgment, corroborated for the sonorant cases by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022), not on K&S. Note: the source does not give an explicit, gemination-specific statement about vowel backing before uvulars/emphatics (the geminate-extension of KAB_A_BACKING/KAB_A_BACKING_PRECEDING rests on phonological reasoning from the p.464 spreading-feature description plus internal consistency with KAB_N_VELAR_ASSIM, not a direct textual citation for geminate contexts).

sources.author: Karaoui, Fazia; Djeradi, Amar; Djeradi, Rachida
sources.title: Acoustic Characterization of the Noise Sources for the Kabyle Fricatives Consonants
sources.notes: Read. Confirms the Kabyle fricative inventory [s, f, ʃ, v, ʒ, z, ħ, h, ʕ, ç, ʝ] (p.123 abstract) — the spirantized velars [ç ʝ] are phonemic surface fricatives — and lists spirantization, labio-velarization, palatalization and affrication as Kabyle-specific features (p.123 §1).

sources.author: Bedar, Amazigh; Quellec, Lucie; Tifrit, Ali
sources.title: Post-sonorant occlusivization in Kabyle
sources.publisher: 19th Meeting of the French Phonology Network (RFP 2022), Porto, Portugal, 7-9 June 2022 (conference presentation)
sources.notes: FULL TEXT READ 2026-07-17 (via user-supplied copy; HAL blocks automated fetch). Kabyle-specific study of post-sonorant occlusivization based on Chemini Kabyle (south-east Bejaïa), with comparative data from Aït Mengellat, Boghni, and Makouda, in an Element-Theory framework. CONFIRMED distribution table (their (6)/(13)): /β/ occlusivizes after n,m but not r,l; /θ,ð/ occlusivize after l,n,m but not r; /ç/ occlusivizes after all of r,l,n,m; /ʝ/ occlusivizes after r,l,n but NOT m. Nasal-cluster forms confirmed: /nβ/→[mb], /nθ,nð/→[nt,nd], /nç,nʝ/→[ŋk,ŋɡ] (homorganic assimilation included). CONFIRMED /mʝ/→[mʝ] as the one m-context non-occlusivization — but this is conditioned on the following vowel: their Table 27-28 shows /mʝ/→[mʝ] before ə/i/a and /mʝ/→[mh] (debuccalized) before u, in Chemini, Boghni, and Makouda alike. CONFIRMED dialectal split for post-/m/ /t,d/-occlusivization: Chemini and Aït Mengellat occlusivize (/mθ/→[mt], /mð/→[md]); Boghni and Makouda do not. /mβ/→[mb] and /mç/→[mk] are constant across all four varieties. NOTE: an earlier revision of this spec miscited this study (wrong author first names, 'BLS 50' venue, eScholarship link, 'four varieties/Table 6' claims) — corrected 2026-07-17, and the underlying phoneme-level claims re-verified against the full paper the same day.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Kabyle language
sources.notes: SECONDARY cross-check (flagged). Used only to confirm the standard 34-letter Berber Latin alphabet grapheme table (c=ʃ, č=t͡ʃ, ǧ=d͡ʒ, j=ʒ, ɣ, x=χ, ḥ=ħ, ɛ=ʕ, q, emphatics ṛ ṣ ḍ ṭ ẓ, ⟨e⟩=schwa, spirantized b d g k t → β ð ʝ ç θ) against the primary phonology sources. NOT used as the source of any phonological claim.

sources.author: Dallet, Jean-Marie
sources.title: Dictionnaire kabyle-français: parler des At Mangellat, Algérie
sources.notes: Lexical source for the TTS gold sentences. Also independently cited by Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) as the reference dictionary for the Aït Mengellat variety compared in that study.

sources.author: Naït-Zerrad, Kamal
sources.title: Grammaire moderne du kabyle / tajerrumt tatrart n teqbaylit
sources.notes: Grammatical/lexical source for the TTS gold sentences.

sources.author: Chaker, Salem
sources.title: Propositions pour la notation usuelle à base latine du berbère
sources.publisher: Centre de Recherche Berbère – INALCO, Paris (synthèse de l'atelier des 24-25 juin 1996)
sources.notes: Direct source for the alphabet assumed by the INPUT CONTRACT of this spec. Explicitly phonological in inspiration: its own consonant chart lists the lax stop/spirant pairs (b/β, t/θ, d/ð, k/ç, g/ʝ) and prescribes writing tension by letter doubling. This is the 'tamaziɣt/INALCO' notation the spec's grapheme table encodes. Independently cited as reference [1] in Karaoui, Djeradi & Djeradi (2024), corroborating its standing as the field's default orthography citation.

sources.author: Mammeri, Mouloud
sources.title: Précis de grammaire berbère (kabyle)
sources.notes: Codified the Latin orthography for Kabyle on which the INALCO 1996 standard and modern usage build; the conventions assumed by the INPUT CONTRACT descend from this work.

sources.author: Souag, Lameen
sources.title: Kabyle in Arabic Script: A History without Standardisation
sources.publisher: In: Creating Standards, De Gruyter, pp. 273-296. DOI 10.1515/9783110639063-011
sources.notes: Documents that the Latin orthography became dominant for Kabyle only in the second half of the 20th century (Mammeri codification, then INALCO 1996 standardization); supports the script assumptions of the INPUT CONTRACT.

sources.author: Wang, Beini
sources.title: Syllabification and Schwa Epenthesis in Kabyle
sources.publisher: McGill Working Papers in Linguistics 26.1 ('Topics in Kabyle Linguistics', guest eds. Baier, Coon & Sonderegger), McGill University
sources.notes: Argues all Kabyle schwas are epenthetic repairs of ill-formed clusters, and that geminates are never broken by epenthesis (e.g. [θaqqraːts] 'bottle') — supports the schwa statement in KNOWN LIMITS and bears on sk-type clusters (the askar question). Confirmed 2026-07-17 to be part of the same McGill 'Topics in Kabyle Linguistics' special issue that also contains Elias (2020) on Kabyle geminate strength, which Bedar, Quellec & Tifrit (2022) themselves cite. NOTE: an internal project report miscited this paper as 'Yuan Wang, The Kabyle Syllable, University of Delaware' — corrected here.

sources.author: MOKRAOUI, Athmane (boffire)
sources.title: Native-speaker verification sessions for the Kabyle orthography2ipa spec
sources.notes: Item-by-item verification tables of 2026-07-16/17 covering spirantization of /b d t k ɡ/ in word-initial, post-vocalic and post-consonant positions. Rulings incorporated in this spec: categorical post-consonant blocking for k/ɡ with the /mʝ/ exception; word-initial /t/ → [θ] before ALL vowels including /i/ (tifinaɣ, tilufa, tilelli, tikwal); rejection of the [ʃ] allophone of /k/; gloss of anta 'which one (f.)'.

sources.author: MOKRAOUI, Athmane (boffire); corpus analysis run 2026-07-17
sources.title: Kabyle corpus audit for cluster attestation and orthographic contamination
sources.notes: 31,085 samples / 21,260 unique words from boffire/tatoeba-en-kab, boffire/nllb_en_kab, boffire/kabyle-english-TM, boffire/kabyle-english-translatewiki. Blocked clusters richly attested (rk 116, lk 100, nk 77, fk 81, tk 103; rg 87, ng 155, zg 63); exotic clusters (xk, qk, zk, ɣk) unattested. Motivated the geminate-context extension of the k/ɡ blocking rules (nettkel, lettkal — k after ⟨tt⟩). Greek/Cyrillic lookalike scan: 25 hits in kabyle-english-translatewiki (to be cleaned at source); other three datasets clean.
